title_en: Author Biography and His Revolutionary Work
content_en:
Abd ar-Rahman ibn Muhammad ibn Khaldun al-Hadrami was born in Tunis on the first of Ramadan 732 AH (27 May 1332 CE) into an aristocratic Andalusian family that had migrated to North Africa after the decline of Muslim rule in Spain. He received an excellent traditional education in Tunis — Quran, Arabic, fiqh, kalam, philosophy, and the mathematical sciences — before embarking on a turbulent career that took him across North Africa, Andalusia, and ultimately to Egypt, where he spent his final decades as a judge and teacher.

Ibn Khaldun's life was marked by extraordinary intellectual achievement and by the volatile politics of the late medieval Maghreb, in which he was an active — and often embattled — participant. He served various North African rulers as a court official, diplomat, and legal advisor, experiencing imprisonment, exile, and sudden reversals of fortune that gave him unparalleled insight into the dynamics of political power and the rise and fall of states.

His masterwork, Al-Muqaddimah (The Introduction or Prolegomena), was composed in 1377 CE during a period of semi-withdrawal at the isolated castle of Ibn Salama in western Algeria. It was intended as the introductory volume of his universal history, Kitab al-Ibar (The Book of Lessons), but its intellectual achievement so far surpassed the subsequent volumes that it became famous independently. Al-Muqaddimah is arguably the most original work of historical and social theory produced in the pre-modern Islamic world and has been called by some modern scholars the first work of sociology, philosophy of history, or social science.

Ibn Khaldun died in Cairo in 808 AH (1406 CE), having revised and expanded Al-Muqaddimah several times. His encounter with Timur (Tamerlane) outside the walls of Damascus in 1401 — during which the two great men met and conversed — is one of the most extraordinary episodes in medieval intellectual history.
